critic_raw:
This SDOH-augmented readmission prediction experiment has a solid baseline design with several important gaps.

First, the comparison between models is not properly isolated for causal attribution. The proposed model uses gradient-boosted decision trees with SDOH + clinical features, while the baseline uses logistic regression with clinical features only. This comparison simultaneously varies both the model architecture (GBDT vs. logistic regression) and the feature set (SDOH+clinical vs. clinical only). To test whether SDOH improves prediction, the appropriate comparison is GBDT+SDOH vs. GBDT+clinical only. The current design cannot attribute any observed performance difference to SDOH features specifically.

Second, the stratified random split on multi-year EHR data ignores temporal clustering at the patient level. Patients with multiple admissions will have all visits in the random pool; a patient's early admissions may be in the test set while later admissions are in training, allowing the model to learn patient-specific patterns that would constitute future leakage. The design should either use patient-level splits (all admissions from a patient in one split) or a chronological split with patient-level consistency.

Third, AUC-ROC is the primary metric but is not directly aligned with the decision-support use case. Hospital readmission prediction models are typically used to flag high-risk patients at discharge for intervention. For this purpose, precision-recall tradeoffs at clinically relevant operating points (e.g., precision at top-5% risk) matter more than aggregate AUC. The design mentions 'supplementary metrics like precision and recall' but does not specify operating points.

Fourth, SDOH features (income, education, housing) have well-documented differential availability by patient demographics—lower-income patients may have more complete SDOH records through social work assessments, while higher-income patients may have no SDOH documentation. This creates a systematic missingness pattern that could confound the SDOH benefit signal.
all_issues_raised: Comparison conflates model architecture and feature set—cannot isolate SDOH benefit
Random split on multi-year EHR allows patient-level temporal leakage
AUC-ROC not aligned with clinical operating point use case
SDOH feature availability is systematically biased by patient demographics

round_transcripts.critic: Four issues: (1) comparison confounds architecture and features; (2) patient-level temporal leakage in random split; (3) AUC-ROC misaligned with clinical operating point use; (4) SDOH availability biased by patient demographics.
round_transcripts.defender:
On architecture/feature confound: This is the most critical flaw and is well-identified. The experiment as described cannot answer whether SDOH improves prediction—it can only answer whether GBDT+SDOH outperforms logistic regression+clinical. Adding a GBDT+clinical condition is essential for a valid conclusion. This is a required design addition.

On patient-level leakage: Valid concern. For patients with multiple admissions, a random split can place earlier admissions in the test set while later admissions train the model—effectively providing future patient-specific trajectory information. Patient-level split (all admissions from a patient in one partition) with chronological ordering within patients would address this.

On AUC-ROC vs. clinical operating point: AUC-ROC is appropriate as a primary ranking metric, but for clinical deployment, precision-recall curves with marked operating points (e.g., top decile precision) are more actionable. Adding precision at top-10% risk as a secondary metric with clinical justification would address this.

On SDOH availability bias: This is a real and subtle confound. If SDOH data is systematically more complete for high-risk or low-income patients, the model may appear to benefit from SDOH features when it is actually benefiting from an indirect signal of social work assessment intensity (which correlates with clinical complexity). This should be analyzed explicitly—reporting SDOH benefit stratified by data completeness level.

round_transcripts.critic: All four issues are conceded. The architecture/feature confound is the most critical—without adding a GBDT+clinical condition, the study cannot support its stated hypothesis. The patient-level leakage is also a real threat to validity. The other two are protocol improvements.
round_transcripts.defender: Agreed. Required changes: (1) add GBDT+clinical-only condition as a proper control for the SDOH benefit; (2) patient-level chronological split; (3) precision at operating point as primary clinical metric alongside AUC; (4) SDOH completeness stratification analysis. With these additions the design is valid for its stated hypothesis.

all_issues_adjudicated: Architecture and feature set confounded—GBDT+clinical condition missing
Patient-level temporal leakage in random EHR split
SDOH availability bias by patient demographics not analyzed
